Prioritizing Practical Mental Health Care in Humanitarian Settings

This title was summarized by AI from the post below.
Peter Ventevogel
2mo Edited
  • Report this post

Mental health support in humanitarian settings should prioritise practical, scalable care over complex diagnostic frameworks and intensive psychotherapies. Our recent correspondence in The Lancet Psychiatry outlines why focusing on the diagnosis and treatment of personality disorders is not feasible during major emergencies. Instead, we advocate for transdiagnostic approaches that employ brief, skills-based interventions to enhance functioning, strengthen emotional regulation, and build resilience across a wide range of mental health conditions. The most effective way to meet diverse needs, including personality-related difficulties, in low-resource and emergency contexts worldwide is to expand workforce capacity through task-sharing approaches within a multi-layered system of care. With the current dramatic funding reductions for humanitarian assistance, we must do everything possible to ensure broad and equitable access to essential mental health and psychosocial support for all who need it. Thanks a lot to Raghda Sleit, Esubalew Haile, Sarah Harrison, Claire Whitney, Phiona Naserian, Zeinab Hijazi, and Fahmy bahgat for the great collaboration on this short piece. https://lnkd.in/exdwDB5N

Mental health needs and personality disorders in humanitarian settings thelancet.com

Jelena Savić Strong, M.A. 2mo
  • Report this comment

This is a powerful argument—but also a dangerous one if taken too far. Framing personality-related difficulties as “neither feasible nor desirable” to address in humanitarian settings risks quietly institutionalising a two-tier system of care: - stabilisation for the many, - actual psychological transformation for the few. Yes—scale matters. Yes—task-sharing and brief, transdiagnostic interventions are indispensable in low-resource environments. But let’s be honest about what we are doing. We are not replacing depth with innovation. We are often replacing depth with what the system can afford to deliver. In contexts shaped by chronic trauma, displacement, violence, and loss, personality patterns are not abstract diagnoses—they are adaptations to prolonged instability. To deliberately step away from engaging them is not just a clinical decision. It is a political and ethical one. Because the message becomes: You are “stabilised enough” to function—but not entitled to fully heal. And that should make all of us uncomfortable. …

Rezhna Mohammed, PhD 2mo
  • Report this comment

Thanks for the share. Strong and timely point. Our forthcoming paper on mental health triage in a displacement context similarly suggests that, in acute emergencies, broad first-stage triage is more useful than narrow diagnostic gatekeeping. The question is less “which disorder first?” and more “who is in distress, who is functionally impaired, and who needs what level of care now?

  • Mohammed Salah Alfahal
    2mo
    • Report this post

    In Sudan, social support is not a concept — it is a way of survival. I am pleased to share my latest publication in Medicine: Exploring the protective role of social support on PTSD, depression, and anxiety among internally displaced persons residing in shelters amidst war in Sudan In Sudanese culture, support is lived — through family bonds, neighbors, shared hardship, and collective resilience. 📊 Our findings showed that higher perceived social support was significantly associated with fewer post-traumatic stress symptoms among internally displaced persons living in shelters in Port Sudan. Why does this matter? 👉 PTSD is closely linked to threat, fear, and disrupted sense of safety 👉 Social support can act as a “social safety signal” — restoring a sense of protection, trust, and emotional regulation In environments of war and displacement, where danger and uncertainty are ongoing, the presence of supportive relationships may help individuals regulate fear, process trauma, and regain a sense of stability. However, this protective effect was less evident for depression and anxiety. This highlights an important insight: 👉 Mental health in displacement is not shaped by social support alone 👉 It is also strongly influenced by social and structural determinants Factors such as unemployment, poverty, prolonged uncertainty, and limited access to care may sustain depression and anxiety — even when social support is present. This challenges purely clinical or individual-focused models of care. It reminds us that: 👉 Mental health is not only treated in clinics 👉 It is shaped by community, culture, and living conditions In contexts of war and displacement, effective responses must combine: • Strengthening community and social support • Addressing structural and socioeconomic stressors • Expanding accessible, culturally grounded MHPSS services In shelters where everything is uncertain, connection remains one of the few constants — but it cannot replace the need for structural change. If we want to support mental health in crisis, we must protect not only individuals — but the social fabric and systems that shape their lives. article link: https://lnkd.in/dKp7X5cC 🔗 DOI: 10.1097/MD.0000000000048343 #MentalHealth #Psychiatry #Sudan #MHPSS #PTSD #SocialSupport #SocialDeterminants #StructuralDeterminants #CommunityResilience #HumanitarianHealth #PublicHealth #ConflictSettings #Research

  • Gama Mananga Ph.D
    2mo Edited
    • Report this post

    This book explores the recovery process from Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) among survivors of the Eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) war, focusing on the psychological, social, and cultural factors influencing healing. Utilizing a mixed-methods approach, data were collected through clinical assessments, interviews, and community surveys across affected regions. Findings highlight the effectiveness of culturally adapted therapeutic interventions, including trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy, narrative exposure therapy, and community-based support systems. The research emphasizes the critical role of social reintegration, local healing practices, and the restoration of a sense of safety in facilitating recovery. Recommendations are provided to guide policymakers, mental health practitioners, and humanitarian organizations in developing comprehensive care strategies to address PTSD in post-conflict settings like the DRC. gamafaith.com

In this piece, I argue that coercion is often framed as necessary for safety or recovery. However, in practice, it can do the opposite, eroding trust, undermining autonomy, and deepening distress. Many of us with lived experience of psychiatric hospitalization have been told that these interventions are “for our own good,” even when they leave lasting harm. 🔗 Read the column here: https://lnkd.in/eJcAJUqd

    “Waiting is often the most harmful part of a mental health claim.” When someone is ready to seek help, delays can quietly make things worse. Long waits for psychiatric assessment can slow recovery and leave patients feeling stuck. This often leads to: • Symptoms escalating while care is delayed • Disconnection from work and daily routines • Uncertainty for GPs managing complex care • Longer and more difficult recovery pathways Earlier specialist input helps bring clarity, direction, and momentum back to care. Dokotela supports GPs with faster access to psychiatry so patients don’t have to wait months to start getting the help they need. 👉 See how faster psychiatric access can support better recovery pathways. https://lnkd.in/gmeKccsq #GeneralPractice #MentalHealthCare #Dokotela #PsychiatryAccess #GPsupport

  • Duke International
    2mo
    • Report this post

    What if the future of mental health care was actually rooted in the past and in community? Professor Thomas Adeoye Lambo, a Nigerian psychiatrist trained in the UK, returned home in the 1950s to challenge the very foundations of Western psychiatric practice. His insight was radical yet elegantly simple: healing cannot happen in isolation from a patient's culture, family, or beliefs. 🔹 He created the Aro Village System, a therapeutic community where patients lived, farmed, and recovered alongside traditional healers and modern doctors. 🔹 He became a founding father of transcultural psychiatry, influencing global mental health frameworks. 🔹 He broke barriers as the first African Vice-Chancellor of the University of Ibadan and later as Deputy Director General of the WHO. Professor Lambo's legacy reminds us that innovation in healthcare doesn't always mean more technology—it often means more humanity, more respect for tradition, and more courage to bridge worlds. Read the full tribute from Duke International Magazine. 👇 https://lnkd.in/eipGAyMZ #MentalHealthLeadership #TransculturalPsychiatry #GlobalHealth #ThomasAdeoyeLambo #DukeIntlMag #HealthEquity #AfricanScholars
